logoAlt: A Long Journey to a New Land logo
title: A Long Journey to a New Land
intro: This powerful exhibit shares the stories of immigrants who traveled far from home in search of opportunity, and the impact they had on building our community.
additionalSponsors: United Steelworkers District 12 and United Steelworkers Local Union 3267
City of Pueblo and Pueblo Water

items.title: Playthe Shift Change Whistle
items.text:
As the "voice of Pueblo," Colorado Fuel & Iron Company's famed shift whistle announced shift changes and lunch breaks for mill workers and kept time for the greater Pueblo community. Originally powered by steam, the whistle was blown several times a day to mark the beginning of each shift at 7:00 am, mid day at 3:00 pm, and the night shift at 11:00 pm. It was also blown at noon to mark the lunch hour. 

As the mill cut back in operations in the 1980s, there was the inability to create the steam necessary to blow the whistle and the company

panels.title: CF&I's Japanese Community
paragraphs: Japanese immigrants began arriving in the United States in the late 19th century, and many made their way to Pueblo during the early 1900s as CF&I expanded. Drawn by the promise of steady employment, they settled primarily in the Bessemer neighborhood, close to the steelworks

The company's growing labor needs created opportunities for Japanese workers, who took positions throughout the mill and mines. Though far from home, they built a supportive community rooted in mutual aid, shared language, and cultural traditions. Families opened shops, participated in neighborhood life, and worked to maintain ties to their heritage while forging a future in southern Colorado.

Despite facing social and economic barriers in the broader United States, Japanese residents in Pueblo found stability in their work and in the friendships formed within and beyond their neighborhood. Their contributions became part of the fabric of the steel mill and the city that grew around it.

panels.title: Ellis Island: Gateway to a New Life
paragraphs:
For millions of immigrants, the first glimpse of the Statue of Liberty rising over New
York Harbor brought overwhelming relief and hope. Political turmoil, economic
hardship, and religious persecution across Europe had driven the largest migration
movement in world history. By the late 1800s, Castle Garden-the nation's original immigration center-could no
longer handle the flood of arrivals, leading to the creation of Ellis Island.
On January 1, 1892, the new federal processing station opened its doors. Over the next 62
years, more than 12 million people entered the United States through this gateway. The
inspection process could be daunting, but for most, it lasted only a few hours before they
were free to begin their new lives.
Though a small number were denied entry and forced to return home, for the vast
majority, Ellis Island represented the beginning of the American dream-a place where hardship met opportunity, and hope carried families forward toward a better future.
